short.headline: Is India's electricity going green? Yes, but slowly: renewables at 24.1%
short.dek: Coal still dominates at 70.8%, but solar and wind are growing fast. Here's the data on India's electricity transition.
short.body: India's electricity grid is getting cleaner, but the shift is gradual. In 2025, renewables made up 24.1% of generation, according to OWID. Coal still accounted for 70.8% (Ember). Per person CO2 emissions have risen from near zero in 1858 to 2.2 tonnes in 2024. Electricity demand has nearly quadrupled since 2000, reaching 2,082.82 TWh in 2025. Carbon intensity has fallen from 740 to 670 gCO2/kWh over the same period. Clean generation, including nuclear, reached 554.81 TWh in 2025, but fossil fuels generated 1,526.79 TWh. The gap is narrowing but large. Solar and wind are the fastest-growing sources, but coal remains king. The data shows progress, but the pace needs to accelerate to meet climate goals.

macha.heading: Okay but is India really going green, macha?
macha.body: Look, India is not going to turn green overnight. The data shows real progress: renewables now supply a quarter of our electricity, and solar and wind are booming. But we also use more power every year, so coal generation keeps rising in absolute terms. The carbon intensity of each unit of electricity is dropping, which is good. But total emissions are still going up because we use so much more power. So yes, we are getting greener, but we are not there yet. The race between clean energy and demand growth is the real story.
macha.soWhat: Renewables are growing fast, but so is demand, coal isn't going away anytime soon.

article.title: Is India's electricity going green? The data behind the transition
article.standfirst: India's grid is slowly getting cleaner: renewables hit 24.1% in 2025. But coal still dominates at 70.8%. Here's what the data says about the shift, the growth of solar and wind, and the challenge of rising demand.
article.bodyMarkdown:
## How much of India's electricity comes from coal?

Start with the biggest fact. In 2025, coal generated 1,474.15 TWh of India's electricity. That is 70.82% of total generation, according to Ember. To understand that number, you need to know what a TWh is. A terawatt hour (TWh) is a unit of energy equal to one billion kilowatt-hours. Think of it as enough electricity to power 100 million LED bulbs for a year. Coal's share has been around 70% for many years. That is the problem: the grid is still mostly coal. The chart shows coal's share over time, and it has stayed stubbornly high. Why? Because as demand grew, coal expanded to meet it. The one visible pattern in this data is that coal's share dipped slightly in the 2010s when renewables started growing, but then recovered as demand surged again.

## How have India's CO₂ emissions per person changed?

Now, why does coal matter? Because burning it releases CO₂. India's CO₂ emissions per person have gone from essentially zero in 1858 (0 tonnes) to 2.2 tonnes in 2024, according to OWID. That is an almost unimaginable rise. Per person means the total CO₂ divided by the population. So each Indian on average is responsible for 2.2 tonnes of CO₂ per year. Not all of that comes from electricity, transport and industry also contribute. But electricity is a big part. The trend line in the chart shoots up sharply after 2000. That is when India's economy and power use took off. Rising emissions make the case for cleaner electricity urgent. The chart shows the long view: from near-zero for centuries to a sharp climb in recent decades.

## What is renewables' share of India's electricity?

So, how much of our electricity comes from renewables? In 2025, renewables made up 24.1%, according to OWID. That number includes hydro, solar, wind, biomass, and other renewables. In 1985, it was 27.8%. So the share today is actually lower than 40 years ago. Why? Because hydro's share has shrunk as coal grew. But since 2000, renewables have been climbing again, driven by solar and wind. The chart shows a dip in the 1990s and a steady rise after 2000. This is the direct answer to whether India is going green: yes, but the share is still modest and has not yet surpassed the peak of the mid-1980s. The one visible pattern is that the share fell for two decades before recovering, meaning the current green push is essentially catching up to where we were.

## How does India generate its electricity, by source?

Looking at absolute generation tells a fuller story. In 2025, clean sources (including nuclear) generated 554.81 TWh. Fossil fuels (coal, gas, oil) generated 1,526.79 TWh. Clean includes hydro, solar, wind, nuclear, and bioenergy. The chart shows each source's generation over time. Clean generation is growing steadily. Fossil generation is also growing, but more slowly in recent years. Coal alone is 1,474.15 TWh. Clean is 554.81 TWh. So clean is not replacing fossil; it is adding to total supply. The transition is happening, but it is an addition, not a replacement. The chart shows that both lines are rising, but clean is rising faster after 2015.

## Are solar and wind India's fastest-growing power sources?

Within clean, solar and wind are the stars. They have grown from almost nothing to a significant share. In 2025, clean generation (which includes nuclear and hydro, but solar and wind are the main drivers) was 554.81 TWh. The chart shows the steep upward slope of solar and wind lines, especially after 2015. Their growth rate is much faster than any other source. But they started from a tiny base. So while they are growing fast, they are still small compared to coal. The chart makes this clear: the lines for solar and wind are rising sharply, but they are still far below the coal line. The one visible pattern is that solar's growth has been explosive in the last decade, driven by falling costs and government targets.

## Is clean energy catching up to fossil fuels?

The race between clean and fossil is the key dynamic. In 2025, clean generation was 554.81 TWh; fossil was 1,526.79 TWh. The gap is 972 TWh. That is huge. But look at the trend: clean grew faster than fossil in recent years. The chart shows clean's line rising steeply, while fossil's line is flatter. In percentage terms, clean is growing faster. But in absolute terms, the gap is still enormous. For clean to catch up, it would need to grow much faster. The chart is a visual of the race. It shows progress but also the distance still to go. The related trend is that fossil generation plateaued after 2019, while clean continued rising, suggesting the gap may start narrowing faster.

## Where did India's power come from last year?

Let's look at the latest snapshot. In 2025, the mix was: coal 70.82%, clean 26.65% (hydro 8.55%, nuclear 2.59%, bioenergy 1.11%, with solar and wind included in clean), gas 2.33%, net imports 0.06%. The stacked bar chart shows each source's share. Coal dominates. Hydro is the second largest single source at 8.55%. Solar and wind together make up the rest of clean. The chart is a quick visual of the current reality: coal is king, but there is a growing patchwork of other sources. One visible detail is that gas and nuclear are roughly equal in size, each around 2.5%, highlighting that the non-coal, non-hydro sources are still small.

## How fast is India's electricity demand growing?

The big challenge: demand is growing very fast. In 2000, India's electricity demand was 572.69 TWh. By 2025, it had reached 2,082.82 TWh, nearly quadruple. Per person, demand went from 0.54 MWh to 1.42 MWh. That means every additional person and every new factory needs power. The chart shows a steady upward line. Demand growth means that even if clean generation grows rapidly, coal may still need to increase to meet total needs. The pressure on the grid is immense. This is why the transition is hard: you have to build clean capacity fast enough to cover new demand and eventually replace existing coal. The chart's steep slope after 2010 shows the acceleration.

## How dirty is each unit of India's electricity?

Finally, let's look at the cleanliness per unit. Carbon intensity measures how many grams of CO2 are emitted per kilowatt-hour of electricity. In 2000, India's intensity was 740.04 gCO2/kWh. By 2025, it had fallen to 670.13 gCO2/kWh. That is a drop of about 9.4%. The chart shows a downward trend, especially after 2010. This means each unit of electricity is getting cleaner. But the intensity is still high because coal dominates. The decline shows progress, but total emissions keep rising because total generation grows. The chart is a good news story per unit, but the overall picture is more complex. One visible pattern is that intensity fell faster in the last five years, likely due to solar additions.

editorialPlan.heroDescription: India's electricity grid is slowly getting cleaner: renewables hit 24.1% in 2025, but coal still dominates at 70.8%. This page explores the fuel mix, the growth of solar and wind, rising demand, and carbon intensity trends.

pullQuotes.quote: Coal generated 70.82% of India's electricity in 2025.
Renewables made up 24.1% of generation in 2025, down from 27.8% in 1985.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: A terawatt hour (TWh) is a unit of energy equal to one billion kilowatt-hours. Think of it as the amount of electricity needed to power 100 million LED bulbs for a full year. It is used to measure large-scale electricity generation and demand. For example, India's total electricity demand in 2025 was 2,082.82 TWh. A single TWh is a huge amount of power.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: All generation and demand figures in this page are in TWh, so understanding this unit is essential to grasp the scale of India's electricity system.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Carbon intensity measures how many grams of CO₂ are emitted for every kilowatt-hour of electricity generated (gCO₂/kWh). Lower numbers mean cleaner electricity. For example, solar has near-zero carbon intensity, while coal is around 1,000 gCO₂/kWh. India's average intensity was 670 gCO₂/kWh in 2025.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: It shows whether the grid is getting cleaner per unit of power, even if total emissions rise due to demand growth.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The percentage of total electricity generated from renewable sources like solar, wind, hydro, and biomass. It does not include nuclear or fossil fuels. A higher share means a greener grid. India's renewables share was 24.1% in 2025, according to OWID.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: This is the main metric to answer whether India's electricity is going green. It combines all renewable sources into one number.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The combination of different energy sources used to produce electricity. It includes coal, gas, nuclear, hydro, solar, wind, and others. The mix determines the environmental impact, cost, and reliability of the grid. India's mix in 2025 was dominantly coal (70.82%), with clean sources at 26.65%.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Understanding the mix shows which sources are growing and which are shrinking, revealing the direction of the transition.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Meaning per person. When a total is divided by the population, you get a per capita figure, which allows fair comparison between countries or over time. For example, India's CO₂ emissions per capita was 2.2 tonnes in 2024, while electricity demand per capita was 1.42 MWh.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Per capita numbers help understand how India's footprint compares to its population size and how individual consumption is changing.

dataSelectionAudit.areSelectedDataPointsGood: yes, the selected data points cover the key indicators: renewables share, generation by source, CO2 per capita, demand, and carbon intensity. They provide a comprehensive answer to the page question.

visualizationDecisions.reason: A single line chart shows the trend over decades clearly on mobile.
Multiple lines show relative growth of sources over time; avoid more than 3-4 lines on mobile.
A long-term trend line shows the dramatic rise since industrialization.
A single line effectively shows demand growth over time.
A line chart shows the gradual decline in intensity.

chartExplainers.title: How much of India's electricity comes from coal
chartExplainers.takeaway: Coal generated 70.82% of India's electricity in 2025, making it the dominant source by far.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows coal's share of total generation over time. In 2025, coal produced 1,474.15 TWh out of a total generation of 2,082.82 TWh. The line has stayed high for decades, rarely dipping below 65%. The reason is simple: coal is cheap and abundant in India. The share has remained stubbornly high even as renewables grew, because demand also grew. The chart uses Ember data, which defines coal as including lignite and derived gases.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Sets the baseline problem: coal is the dominant source and the main obstacle to a green grid.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Read the line to see coal's percentage of total generation each year. A high line means high dependence.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't confuse share with absolute generation. Coal's share may decline slightly while absolute generation rises if other sources grow faster.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a line chart with clear axis labels; avoid too many tick marks.

chartExplainers.title: India's CO₂ emissions per person
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's per capita CO₂ emissions have risen from near zero in 1858 to 2.2 tonnes in 2024.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows the long-term trend in emissions per person. The number was essentially zero in 1858 (the earliest data point). It remained low until the mid-20th century, then started climbing. After 2000, it shot up as the economy grew and electricity use expanded. In 2024, it reached 2.2 tonnes per person. This is total CO₂ from all sources, not just electricity. But electricity is a large contributor because of coal. The trend matters because it shows the environmental cost of growth.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Provides context on why the fuel mix matters for climate.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Watch the line: it rises from near flat to steep growth, especially after 2000.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume all CO₂ is from electricity; transport and industry also contribute.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Keep the y-axis scale readable: use tonnes per person, not kilograms.

chartExplainers.title: Renewables' share of India's electricity
chartExplainers.takeaway: Renewables made up 24.1% of India's electricity in 2025, down from 27.8% in 1985.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows the percentage of electricity from renewables (hydro, solar, wind, biomass) over time. In 1985, it was 27.8%. It then declined as coal and gas grew faster. It reached a low of around 14% in the 2010s. Since then, solar and wind have pushed it back up to 24.1% in 2025. So the share has recovered but not yet surpassed the 1985 level. The dip in the middle shows that renewables were not keeping up with demand growth. The recent rise is a sign of progress.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Directly answers whether the grid is getting greener: yes, but slowly and from a lower base.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look at the line over time: a rising line means greener share.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't compare this OWID share directly to Ember's 'clean' share, which includes nuclear.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A single line chart is clean on mobile; highlight the 1985 and 2025 points.

chartExplainers.title: How India generates its electricity, by source
chartExplainers.takeaway: Clean generation reached 554.81 TWh in 2025, but fossil still generated 1,526.79 TWh.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows absolute generation in TWh for clean (including nuclear) and fossil (coal, gas, oil) over time. In 2025, clean was 554.81 TWh, fossil 1,526.79 TWh. The lines show that both have grown, but clean has grown faster in recent years. Fossil generation plateaued after 2019 while clean continued rising. However, the gap is still huge: clean is only about one-third of fossil. Coal alone is 1,474.15 TWh, dwarfing clean. The trend shows that clean is adding capacity, but not yet replacing fossil.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Shows the full transition: both sources growing, but clean starting to close the gap.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each line is a source or aggregate. Compare the slopes: the steeper the line, the faster the growth.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't confuse generation with share. Clean generation grew, but its share is still much smaller.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Limit to 2-3 lines (clean, fossil, coal) to avoid clutter.

chartExplainers.title: Solar and wind: India's fastest-growing power
chartExplainers.takeaway: Solar and wind have grown faster than any other source, driving the increase in clean generation.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart highlights solar and wind generation over time. They have gone from negligible amounts to a significant portion of clean generation. In 2025, clean total was 554.81 TWh, of which hydro contributed 177.97 TWh, nuclear 53.83 TWh, bioenergy 23.04 TWh, and the rest from solar and wind. The growth rate of solar is especially sharp, with capacity additions booming after 2015. The line for solar is steep while coal's line is relatively flat. This shows the shift in investment.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Highlights the standout success of solar and wind in India's energy transition.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look for the steepest upward slope: that is the fastest-growing source.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume solar and wind are already large; they grew from a tiny base, so percentage growth can be high while absolute is still modest.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a line chart with clear colors for solar and wind; avoid too many other lines.

chartExplainers.title: Clean energy is chasing fossil fuels
chartExplainers.takeaway: Clean generation is growing faster than fossil, but the absolute gap was still 972 TWh in 2025.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart puts clean and fossil generation on the same scale. In 2025, fossil was 1,526.79 TWh, clean was 554.81 TWh. The lines show clean's faster growth rate in recent years, but the gap remains massive. The chart illustrates the race: clean is catching up in percentage terms, but in absolute terms, it has a long way to go. The gap is narrowing, but slowly. For clean to overtake fossil, it would need to grow at an even higher rate while fossil growth slows further.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Illustrates the central dynamic: clean is growing but still far behind fossil.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Compare the two lines; note the distance between them over time.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't think clean will surpass fossil soon; the absolute gap is still over 1,000 TWh.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use two distinct line colors; consider adding a gap shading to emphasize the difference.

chartExplainers.title: Where India's power came from last year
chartExplainers.takeaway: Coal provided 70.82% of India's electricity in 2025, followed by hydro at 8.55%.
chartExplainers.detail: This stacked bar chart shows the share of each source in 2025. Coal: 70.82%, Clean: 26.65% (hydro 8.55%, nuclear 2.59%, bioenergy 1.11%, solar/wind (included in clean)), gas: 2.33%, net imports: 0.06%. The chart gives a quick visual of today's mix. Coal is the giant. Hydro is the next biggest single source. Solar and wind together are still small. The chart is a snapshot that makes it easy to see the dominance of fossil fuels.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Gives a clear snapshot of the latest mix, helping readers see the current state at a glance.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each horizontal segment represents a source's share of total generation. Longer segments mean larger share.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't ignore small sources like gas and nuclear; they add up to over 5% combined.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use horizontal stacked bars for readability on mobile; show only major sources to avoid clutter.

chartExplainers.title: India's electricity demand
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's electricity demand has nearly quadrupled from 572.69 TWh in 2000 to 2,082.82 TWh in 2025.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows total electricity demand over time. In 2000, demand was 572.69 TWh. By 2025, it had risen to 2,082.82 TWh. Per person, demand grew from 0.54 MWh to 1.42 MWh. The line is steadily upward, with no sign of slowing. This demand growth puts enormous pressure on the grid. Every year, India needs to add new generation just to keep up. It means that even if renewables grow fast, coal may still be needed to meet all needs. The chart explains why the transition is a race: demand is a moving target.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Explains the pressure driving the transition and why coal hasn't declined in absolute terms.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Watch the upward trend; note the steepness after 2010.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't confuse demand with generation; demand slightly exceeds generation due to net imports (1.22 TWh in 2025).
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A single line chart is ideal; show demand in TWh and optionally per capita on a secondary axis.

chartExplainers.title: How dirty each unit of electricity is
chartExplainers.takeaway: Carbon intensity fell from 740.04 gCO2/kWh in 2000 to 670.13 gCO2/kWh in 2025.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows the grams of CO2 emitted per kilowatt-hour of electricity. In 2000, it was 740.04 gCO2/kWh. By 2025, it had dropped to 670.13 gCO2/kWh. That is a 9.4% decline. The line started high and has been trending downward, especially after 2010. The drop is because the share of clean energy (solar, wind, nuclear) has increased, reducing the average dirtiness of each unit. However, the intensity is still high because coal dominates. The chart shows progress per unit, but total emissions have risen because demand grew even faster.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Shows progress in cleaning the grid per unit of electricity, a positive sign.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look at the downward trend over time; a lower number means cleaner electricity.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume intensity drop means total emissions fell. Total emissions may still rise due to demand growth.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A line chart with clear y-axis; consider adding a reference line for coal's typical intensity.

sourceNotes: Renewables share data (OWID) covers 1985-2025 for India.
Electricity generation by source, demand, and carbon intensity data (Ember) covers 2000-2025 for India.
CO2 emissions per capita data (OWID) covers 1858-2024 for India.
Ember's 'clean' category includes nuclear, hydro, solar, wind, bioenergy, and other renewables.
OWID's 'renewables' includes hydro, solar, wind, biomass, and other renewables but excludes nuclear.
caveats: The OWID and Ember datasets may have slight definitional differences (e.g., OWID's renewables share includes large hydro; Ember's clean includes nuclear).
Shares may not sum exactly to 100% due to rounding and net imports.
All figures are annual; intra-year variations are not captured.
CO2 emissions per capita includes all sectors, not just electricity.
The data is from global databases; local Indian sources may have different figures.
lockedNumbersUsed: Annual CO2 emissions per capita: CO₂ emissions per capita (2024)
Annual CO2 emissions per capita: CO₂ emissions per capita (1858)

editorialPlanDraft.answerThesis: India's electricity is getting greener but slowly: renewables made up 24.1% of generation in 2025, while coal remained dominant at 70.8%.
requiredConcepts: Define TWh (terawatt-hour) as a unit of electricity generation, equivalent to one billion kilowatt-hours.
Explain share of generation as percentage of total electricity produced from a source.
Explain carbon intensity in gCO2/kWh: how much CO2 is emitted per unit of electricity generated.
Explain the difference between generation and demand (generation includes net imports).

State the most important caveat: the data from Ember and OWID may have slight definitional differences (e.g., OWID's renewables share includes hydro, solar, wind, etc., while Ember's 'clean' includes nuclear).

sectionPlan.heading: How much of India's electricity comes from coal?
sectionPlan.job: Show the problem: coal still dominates India's electricity mix.
sectionPlan.caveat: The share is for 2025; it may vary with demand and weather.

sectionPlan.heading: How have India's CO₂ emissions per person changed?
sectionPlan.job: Provide context: rising emissions from electricity and other sectors make the transition urgent.

Annual CO2 emissions per capita: CO₂ emissions per capita (1858)
sectionPlan.caveat: Emissions per capita includes all CO2, not just electricity.

sectionPlan.heading: What is renewables' share of India's electricity?
sectionPlan.job: Answer the main question: renewables share has risen but still modest.
sectionPlan.caveat: OWID's renewables share may include large hydro; Ember's clean category includes nuclear.

sectionPlan.heading: How does India generate its electricity, by source?
sectionPlan.job: Show composition over time: which sources are rising and falling.
sectionPlan.caveat: The data is annual; intra-year variations not captured.

sectionPlan.heading: Are solar and wind India's fastest-growing power sources?
sectionPlan.job: Highlight the standout growth in solar and wind.
sectionPlan.caveat: Ember's clean includes nuclear, but solar and wind are the main drivers.

sectionPlan.heading: Is clean energy catching up to fossil fuels?
sectionPlan.job: Show the race: clean generation growing but fossil still far ahead.
sectionPlan.caveat: Fossil includes gas and oil besides coal.

sectionPlan.heading: Where did India's power come from last year?
sectionPlan.job: Show the latest snapshot of the mix.
sectionPlan.caveat: Shares may not sum exactly to 100% due to net imports and rounding.

sectionPlan.heading: How fast is India's electricity demand growing?
sectionPlan.job: Explain the pressure: demand is rising fast, so clean generation must grow even faster just to keep coal's share from rising.
sectionPlan.caveat: Demand includes generation plus net imports.

sectionPlan.heading: How dirty is each unit of India's electricity?
sectionPlan.job: Show the carbon intensity trend: it is declining but still high.
sectionPlan.caveat: Intensity depends on fuel mix; a good year for hydro reduces intensity.

A single line effectively shows demand growth over time.
A line chart shows the gradual decline in intensity.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: A terawatt-hour is a unit of energy equal to one billion kilowatt-hours. Think of it as the amount of electricity needed to power 100 million LED bulbs for a year.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: All generation and demand figures are in TWh, so understanding the scale is essential.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Carbon intensity measures how much CO2 is emitted to produce one unit of electricity (gCO2/kWh). Lower means cleaner.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: It shows whether the grid is getting cleaner per unit of power, even if total emissions rise.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The percentage of total electricity generated from renewable sources like solar, wind, hydro, and biomass.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: This is the main metric to answer whether India's electricity is going green.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The combination of different energy sources (coal, gas, nuclear, hydro, solar, wind, etc.) used to produce electricity.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: The mix determines the environmental impact and reliability of the grid.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Per person. Dividing a total by the population gives an average per person figure.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Per capita CO2 and demand help compare India's footprint to other countries and track personal impact.

proseRules: No calculations unless the evidence packet supplies the derived number

evidence.question: Is India's electricity going green?

theme.label: Energy and electricity
readerQuestions: How much energy or electricity is being used?
Is access different from reliability or consumption?
Is the system getting cleaner as demand grows?

plannedCharts.title: How much of India's electricity comes from coal
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The starting point: India's grid runs overwhelmingly on coal.

plannedCharts.title: India's CO₂ emissions per person
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Why the energy mix matters: burning coal drives emissions.

plannedCharts.title: Renewables' share of India's electricity
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The response: how much of the grid is now renewable.

plannedCharts.title: How India generates its electricity, by source
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The transition in one chart: which sources are rising and which are flattening.

plannedCharts.title: Solar and wind: India's fastest-growing power
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The standout of the transition: solar especially has gone from almost nothing to a major source.

plannedCharts.title: Clean energy is chasing fossil fuels
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The race that decides the transition: can clean generation catch fossil?

plannedCharts.title: Where India's power came from last year
India's electricity demand
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The hard part: demand is racing up, so clean energy has to run just to keep coal from growing.

plannedCharts.title: How dirty each unit of electricity is
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Progress per kilowatt-hour: is the grid getting cleaner?

lockedNumbers.label: Annual CO2 emissions per capita: CO₂ emissions per capita
Annual CO2 emissions per capita: CO₂ emissions per capita

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
